title: Environmental Clearance (EC) in India
focus: EIA Notification 2006, project categorization, and Supreme Court rulings on ex post facto clearance.
rationale: A distinct, highly testable concept regarding environmental jurisprudence and administrative categorization of projects.
sectionExcerpt: EC is required for projects significantly impacting the environment under the EIA Notification, 2006. Projects are categorized into Category A (Central level, MoEF&CC) and Category B (State level, SEIAA). Prior clearance is mandatory, and the Supreme Court (Common Cause v. UoI, 2017) ruled that ex post facto or retrospective clearance is alien to environmental jurisprudence.
evidenceBullets: EIA Notification 2006 lists over 39 activities requiring prior EC.
Category A projects are handled by MoEF&CC at the Central level.
Category B projects are handled by SEIAA at the State level.
Supreme Court held in Common Cause v. UoI (2017) that ex post facto environmental clearance is invalid.

title: Great Nicobar Island Project (GNIP) and Tribal Rights
focus: Infrastructure project components, nodal agencies, and the conflict between PAT 1956 and FRA 2006 regarding tribal rights.
rationale: Combines geography (Galathea Bay, Ramsar site), tribal governance (PAT vs FRA), and major infrastructure initiatives, making it a prime target for multi-statement UPSC questions.
sectionExcerpt: The GNIP is a massive Greenfield infrastructure project conceived by NITI Aayog, with ANIIDCO as the nodal agency. It includes a transshipment port at Galathea Bay, an airport, a power plant, and a township. A conflict exists over forest land diversion, with the administration citing the Protection of Aboriginal Tribes Act, 1956 (PAT) to bypass the Forest Rights Act, 2006 (FRA) which mandates Gram Sabha consent. Galathea Bay is a Ramsar Site and nesting site for the Leatherback Sea Turtle.
evidenceBullets: Tribal Councils in A&N Islands have limited advisory/executive powers, unlike Sixth Schedule autonomous councils.
PAT 1956 allows unilateral forest land diversion, conflicting with FRA 2006's mandate for Gram Sabha consent.
GNIP was conceived by NITI Aayog; ANIIDCO is the nodal agency.
Project includes an International Transshipment Port at Galathea Bay.
Galathea Bay is a Ramsar Site and a key nesting site for the endangered Leatherback Sea Turtle.

title: Dam Safety in India and Sedimentation Vulnerabilities
focus: Dam statistics, sedimentation issues, Dam Safety Act 2021, and related national/international initiatives.
rationale: Contains rich factual data (India's global rank, largest reservoir) and institutional frameworks (Dam Safety Act, DRIP, ICOLD) highly relevant for Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: India is the 3rd largest dam-owning nation, with Maharashtra having the highest number of large dams and Indira Sagar being the largest by volume. Many reservoirs have lost nearly 50% capacity due to sedimentation. The Dam Safety Act, 2021 established the NCDS, NDSA, SCDS, and SDSO. Initiatives include DRIP (funded by World Bank and AIIB), SHAISYS, DHARMA, and the National Centre for Earthquake Safety of Dams at MNIT Jaipur.
evidenceBullets: India is the 3rd largest dam-owning nation (after China and USA), with Maharashtra having the highest number of large dams.
Indira Sagar Dam (Narmada River, MP) is India's largest reservoir by volume.
Dam Safety Act, 2021 created a 4-tier institutional framework: NCDS, NDSA, SCDS, and SDSO.
DRIP Phase II and III are implemented with financial assistance from the World Bank and AIIB.
National Centre for Earthquake Safety of Dams is located at MNIT Jaipur.
World Commission on Dams was established by the World Bank and IUCN in 1998.

title: Seed Management and Draft Seeds Bill, 2025
focus: Key provisions of the Draft Seeds Bill, 2025, including mandatory registration and the SATHI portal.
rationale: Legislative updates in agriculture are a staple in UPSC exams. The SATHI portal and exemptions for farmers' varieties are key testable facts.
sectionExcerpt: The Draft Seeds Bill, 2025 seeks to replace the Seeds Act, 1966, and Seeds (Control) Order, 1983 to regulate seed quality. It mandates that no unregistered seed can be sold, excepting farmers' varieties and export-only seeds. It requires mandatory onboarding on the SATHI portal (created by MoAFW and NIC) for complete seed traceability. Central and State Seed Committees will be constituted.
evidenceBullets: Draft Seeds Bill, 2025 replaces the Seeds Act, 1966, and Seeds (Control) Order, 1983.
Un-registered seeds cannot be sold, except for farmers' varieties and seeds produced exclusively for export.
SATHI portal (MoAFW + NIC) ensures complete traceability of seeds from origin to farmer.
Seed varieties must conform to the Indian Minimum Seed Certification Standards.
